---
title: "Client Report - Missing values"
subtitle: "Unit 2 Task 3"
author: "Mitzi Vite"
format:
  html:
    self-contained: true
    page-layout: full
    title-block-banner: true
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-location: body
    number-sections: false
    html-math-method: katex
    code-fold: true
    code-summary: "Show the code"
    code-overflow: wrap
    code-copy: hover
    code-tools:
        source: false
        toggle: true
        caption: See code
execute: 
  warning: false
    
---

```{python}
import polars as pl
import requests #new for reading in JSON
import io       #new for reading in JSON
import numpy as np
from lets_plot import *

LetsPlot.setup_html(isolated_frame=True)
```


```{python}
url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/byuidatascience/data4missing/master/data-raw/flights_missing/flights_missing.json"
response = requests.get(url)
df = pl.read_json(io.BytesIO(response.content))
print(df.describe())
```

## QUESTION 1

1. __Convert values to missing__ Start with a fresh copy of the flights dataset by reading it in from the link provided above (as opposed to starting with the dataset you ended with in the last assignment). Identify data values that are problematic, non-sense or impossible and change them to be missing. In your report include one record example (one row) from your clean data, in the raw JSON format. Your example should display at least one missing value so that we can verify it was done correctly. (Note: JSON will convert NaN's to null). Describe your process for finding values that needed to be changed, and how you changed them.

_To identify problematic values, I used unique() on the month column and found "n/a" as an invalid value, which was converted to null. I also checked all numeric columns for negative values and found 40 in num_of_delays_late_aircraft and 17 in minutes_delayed_nas, both of which are impossible values and were also converted to null. Additionally, "Febuary" was corrected to "February". The JSON record above confirms that the conversions were applied correctly, as shown by "month": null."_

```{python}
# Include and execute your code here
print(df["month"].unique().sort())
num_cols = [col for col in df.columns if df[col].dtype in [pl.Float64, pl.Int64]]
for col in num_cols:
    count = df.filter(pl.col(col) < 0).shape[0]
    if count > 0:
        print(f"{col}: {count} negative values")

df = df.with_columns(
  pl.when(pl.col("month")=="n/a")
  .then(None)
  .otherwise(pl.col("month"))
  .alias("month")
)

df = df.with_columns(
  pl.when(pl.col("num_of_delays_late_aircraft") < 0)
  .then(None)
  .otherwise(pl.col("num_of_delays_late_aircraft"))
  .alias("num_of_delays_late_aircraft")
)

df = df.with_columns(
  pl.when(pl.col("minutes_delayed_nas") < 0)
  .then(None)
  .otherwise(pl.col("minutes_delayed_nas"))
  .alias("minutes_delayed_nas")
)

df = df.with_columns(
  pl.col("month").str.replace("Febuary", "February").alias("month")
)

print(df.filter(pl.col("month").is_null()).head(1).write_json())

```

## QUESTION 2

2. __Filling in missing data__ For all the "minutes_delayed" columns, fill in any missing values with the median of that column. For the "num_of" columns, fill in missing values with that column's mean.

_Nothing to write/report for this question_

```{python}
# Include and execute your code here
minute_cols = [col for col in df.columns if col.startswith("minutes_delayed")]
print(minute_cols)

num_of_cols = [col for col in df.columns if col.startswith("num_of")]
print(num_of_cols)

df = df.with_columns(
  [pl.col(col).cast(pl.Float64, strict=False) for col in num_of_cols]
)

df = df.with_columns(
    [pl.col(col).fill_null(pl.col(col).median()) for col in minute_cols]
)

df = df.with_columns(
    [pl.col(col).fill_null(pl.col(col).mean()) for col in num_of_cols]
)
```

## QUESTION 3
 
3. __Display a histogram for num_of_delays_carrier and for minutes_delayed_nas (2 separate histograms in total). Comment on the shape of each one. Also report the mean of each of these columns now that missing values have been filled in.__

_The histogram shows a right-skewed distribution. The most common value is approximately 700 delayed flights per month due to the carrier, with a mean of 691 delays. The long tail to the right indicates that while most months have a moderate number of carrier delays, some airports or months experience significantly higher delays._

```{python}
print(df["num_of_delays_carrier"].mean())
print(df["minutes_delayed_nas"].mean())
# Include and execute your code here

(
  ggplot(df, aes(x="num_of_delays_carrier")) +
  geom_histogram() +
  labs(title='Histogram of Delays by Carrier',
       x='Number of Delays by Carrier',
       y='Frequency')
)

```

_The histogram shows a right-skewed distribution. The data ranges from 0 to 600,000 minutes, but the most frequent values are concentrated near the lower end of the range. The mean of 69,682 minutes is pulled to the right by the extreme values on the right side of the distribution, making it higher than what most airports typically experience in a month._

```{python}
(
  ggplot(df, aes(x="minutes_delayed_nas")) +
  geom_histogram() +
  labs(title="Histogram of Minutes Delayed by NAS",
       x='Minutes Delayed by NAS',
       y='Frequency')

)
```
